content:
1. Introduction
The BPCL Refineries, including Mumbai Refinery (MR), Kochi Refinery (KR), and Bina
Refinery (BR), operate in diverse climatic conditions. This necessitates effective protection
of static equipment and structures against weathering. MR and KR, located in coastal
regions, face a very humid and corrosive environment, while BR, situated centrally,
experiences moderate climatic conditions. To extend the life of the equipment and
structures and improve their aesthetics, painting and coating are essential to prevent
corrosion.
As painting technology rapidly evolves, it has become crucial across various sectors. The
different environmental factors at each refinery require a comprehensive guide on painting
practices. Current painting and coating practices offer significant room for improvement in
quality control, surface preparation, paint selection, and workmanship. Consequently, there
is a need for a unified painting manual for all BPCL refineries, incorporating new
specifications, the latest technological advancements, and user-friendly guidelines.
This specification aims to provide updated knowledge on surface preparation, types of
paints, and equipment-specific painting for internal and external surfaces, including Tanks,
Civil Structures and Buildings. Adopting the recommended painting schedules /
frequencies will ensure high-quality results and mitigate the adverse effects of
environmental aging.
To achieve synergy across the refineries, it is essential to standardize specifications, paint
materials, inspection procedures, quality assurance, and application methods. Thus,
creating a comprehensive "Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries"
for above three refineries is a prudent step.
2. Scope Overview
This Technical Specification of Paint Coatings is designed to serve as an accessible
reference guide/recommended practice for inspection, design, projects and maintenance
engineers. This document outlines painting procedures/specifications for all BPCL
Refineries (MR, KR & BR). General guidelines on paint material selection, application,
inspection, and repair methodologies are provided, along with specific paint and protection
coating systems.
When ordering materials/service contracts, original/updated documents & drawings related
to specific equipment should be consulted in conjunction with the recommendations
provided. Despite our best efforts to compile accurate and organized information, some
minor details have been generalized to meet technical requirements and standard
practices.

content:
We hope this painting specification proves useful in ensuring the reliability of static
equipment, protection against corrosion, and aesthetic improvements. As painting
technology advances, necessary revisions will be incorporated in future editions. We
extend our gratitude to everyone who contributed to this ongoing improvement process.
It details the requirements and recommendations for surface preparation and the
application of external painting and coating on metallic components exposed to
atmospheric conditions, regardless of insulation. While it does not restrict the use of
improved paint systems, such systems should be referred to the Inspection Department
before use.
3. Reference Codes & Standards
Latest editions of the following codes and standards are applicable for the work covered
by this specification: The Contractor is responsible for maintaining the latest editions of all
reference standards and codes at the work site, at their own cost. Compliance with the
paint Manufacturer’s instructions is mandatory, particularly in:
1) Storing paints to prevent exposure to adverse climatic conditions and extreme
temperatures.
2) Adhering to specified shelf-life guidelines.
3) Properly preparing surfaces before painting.
4) Correctly mixing and thinning paints.
5) Following recommended application techniques and time intervals between coats.

content:
4. Criteria for Paint Coatings
The primary criteria for selecting paint or coatings are based on the environment or
atmosphere surrounding the structure or item to be protected or service of the
equipment/asset. Following points should be considered for selection of paint/coatings:
1) Whether the environment is wet or dry. If wet, determine if it is due to fresh water or salt
water.
2) Potential chemical exposure or contamination from acids or alkalis.
3) Consistency of the environment or atmosphere throughout the structure.
4) Exposure to weathering factors such as heat, cold, daily or seasonal temperature
changes, precipitation, wind, sunlight, or harmful solar rays.
5) The importance of aesthetic appearance versus corrosion protection.
4.1. Materials
1) All paints and paint materials must be sourced from approved manufacturers.
2) They should be supplied in the original containers, clearly marked with the contents
description. This includes the specification number, colour reference number,
intended application method, batch number, date of manufacture, shelf-life/expiry
date, and the Manufacturer’s name or trademark.
3) Different brands or types of paints should not be mixed.
4) Paints and coating materials must be stored and prepared according to the
Manufacturer’s instructions.
5) The BPCL may take samples of the paint being used at any time for testing. If a
sample fails to meet the required specifications, the Contractor must remove the
paint from the affected areas and re-apply paint that meets the specifications.
4.2. Paint Approval Requirements
The selection of paint or coating systems intended for use in industrial or critical
service locations shall be subject to prior written approval from BPCL. The
Contractor must submit comprehensive documentation for each proposed
product before any procurement activity. This submission shall include, but is not
limited to, the following:
• Product Data Sheets (PDS)
• Third-Party Test Reports for materials
• Confirmation of Product Data Sheet availability on the Manufacturer’s official
website
• Documented Proven Track Record for similar applications or service conditions
• Approvals from reputed consultants (e.g. EIL)
• Approvals from other Oil & Gas companies
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > PART 9 Field method for the conductometric determination of water- soluble salts > 4 Criteria for Paint Coatings > 4.2 Paint Approval Requirements

content:
• Paint system test reports from accredited third-party laboratories, conducted in
accordance with the “Pre - Qualification Testing” procedures specified in the
relevant section of this specification.
BPCL reserves the right to request trial samples for performance evaluation, if deemed
necessary by the Inspection team. No paint product shall be purchased or applied
without formal written approval from BPCL, ensuring full compliance with the owner’s
technical standards and service requirements.
4.3. Painting Contractor Responsibilities
The painting Contractor shall be responsible for:
1) Prior Qualifications of all Painters and Blasters through Manufacturer and BPCL
before start of job and conducting demonstrations on the test panels.
2) Ensuring high-quality workmanship that complies with this document and all other
relevant regulations, including site rules, safety protocols, and applicable standards
and codes.
3) Obtaining the Owner's assurance that the equipment is certified safe and available,
and that all necessary tests have been completed before beginning any painting
work.
4) Protecting all equipment, structures, and surrounding areas from mechanical
damage, environmental damage, abrasives during blast cleaning, paint droppings,
or overspray.
5) Properly grounding (earthing) all electrical equipment.
6) Taking precautions to avoid interfering with the work of other Contractors.
7) Verifying the characteristics of paints and paint materials obtained from the
manufacturer, using separate technical and health and safety data sheets.
8) Managing waste generated from painting and coating activities in accordance with
site regulations and contract requirements.
9) Maintaining workshop facilities, tools, and equipment in good, clean condition. This
includes regularly cleaning spray guns, brushes, rollers, and paint pots to ensure
they are fit for purpose.
10) Preparing a work plan that aligns with the owner's schedule.
11) Ensuring all personnel are adequately trained and qualified for the tasks they
perform, and that they adhere to safety and quality standards.
12) Conducting regular inspections and quality control checks to ensure compliance
with specifications and standards.
13) Maintaining clear and accurate guide of all work performed, including daily logs,
inspection reports, and material usage records.
14) Coordinating with the owner and other stakeholders to provide progress updates
and address any issues promptly.
15) Ensuring the availability of all necessary Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) for
workers and enforcing its use.
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > PART 9 Field method for the conductometric determination of water- soluble salts > 4 Criteria for Paint Coatings > 4.3 Painting Contractor Responsibilities

content:
16) Addressing and rectifying any defects or deficiencies in the painting work promptly
and to the satisfaction of the owner.
17) Implementing measures to minimize the environmental impact of painting activities,
such as using low-VOC (volatile organic compounds) paints and properly disposing
of hazardous materials.
18) Ensuring that all painting and coating activities comply with relevant environmental
regulations and guidelines.
19) Providing warranty and post-completion support as specified in the contract,
including addressing any issues that arise during the warranty period.
20) Deployment of Certified Quality Control Inspector: For all major works
including, but not limited to, projects, plant painting, tankage Maintenance &
Inspection (M&I), and shutdown-related activities, the Contractor shall deploy a
NACE-certified inspector to serve as the designated Quality Control (QC) Inspector
for painting works. This deployment shall be in addition to the regular site personnel
such as supervisors and site in-charges and shall be mandatory to ensure
compliance with applicable quality standards and specifications. He shall be
responsible for quality work, performing all the quality checks and maintaining
necessary documentation required for painting works.
21) Every painting Contractor shall mobilize painting inspection equipments like WFT
gauge, DFT meter, Surface Profile Gauge, Digital Hygrometer (Humidity Meter),
Bresle salt patch & salt contamination meter, Holiday Tester, Adhesion Tester, Dust
contamination tape, distilled water for copper abrasives salt content check etc.
4.4. Qualification of Painters and Blasters:
Prior to commencement of any surface preparation or painting activities, it is
mandatory that all personnel designated as painters and blasters undergo a formal
qualification process. This process shall be jointly conducted and approved by
the paint manufacturer and BPCL to ensure compliance with quality standards and
application procedures.
Qualification Process:
1) Verification of Experience and Training:
• Each painter and blaster must submit documentation of prior experience and
relevant training certifications (if any).
• The manufacturer/BPCL shall assess the competency of personnel based on
their familiarity with the specified coating system, surface preparation
techniques, quality control parameters etc.
• If painters and blasters hold valid certifications from recognized
organizations, these may be accepted without requiring a demonstration on
test panels, subject to the discretion of the Engineer-in-Charge (EIC).
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > PART 9 Field method for the conductometric determination of water- soluble salts > 1 Verification of Experience and Training:

content:
2) Demonstration on Test Panels:
• Painters and blasters who do not possess prior certification shall be required
to perform a practical demonstration on designated test panels to verify their
competency.
• The demonstration shall include surface preparation (e.g. blasting) and
application of the coating system as per the specification.
• The test panels will be evaluated for surface profile, cleanliness, coating
thickness, and overall workmanship.
• Upon successful demonstration, the manufacturer or BPCL shall jointly
approve the personnel for field execution.
• A record of qualified personnel shall be maintained and made available for
inspection.
Note: No painter or blaster shall be permitted to work on-site unless they have been
formally qualified through this process. This ensures consistent quality, adherence to
specification, and long-term performance of the coating system.
5. Surface Preparation
Most metallic articles that receive protective coatings require thorough cleaning treatments
before application to ensure optimal performance and durability. Surface preparation is
critical, as studies have consistently shown its significant influence on coating effectiveness
compared to the quality of the coating itself.
Surface contaminants such as rust, scale, oil, grease, and dirt must be completely removed
before painting. Invisible contaminants, including soldering fluxes, hand marks, chlorides
from marine atmospheres, and sulphides from industrial environments, must also be
addressed.
For effective paint performance, surfaces to be painted must be thoroughly dry, free from
burrs, weld spatter, flux, rust, loose scale, dirt, dust, grease, oil, and other foreign
substances. When surfaces are exposed to polluted atmospheres, such as salt-laden
environments, they should be washed down with clean, fresh water before blast or power
tool cleaning.
After preparing the substrate surface, any grit or dust should be removed, and a primer
coat applied within four hours to prevent corrosion or recontamination. Surface preparation
should primarily involve dry blast cleaning whenever possible.
The external surfaces of RCC, chimneys, Cooling Towers, buildings etc. should be dry and
clean, with any loose particles of sand, cement, or aggregate removed by scrubbing or, if
necessary, acid etching with a 10-15% HCL solution. After etching, thorough washing with
water is essential to remove acid and loose particles, ensuring complete surface drying
before paint application.

content:
5.3. Surface Preparation by Blast Cleaning
5.3.1. General
Before blast cleaning, all oil, grease, and dirt must be removed using suitable
solvents, steam cleaning, alkaline cleaning agents if necessary, or water jetting.
Excessive layers of rust should be chipped away, and weld spatter and sharp edges
removed.
5.3.2. Blast Cleaning Equipment
1) Compressed air used for blast cleaning must be free of water and oil. Adequate
separators and traps should be installed at the coolest part of the system,
regularly emptied to prevent water and oil carryover. Accumulations of oil and
moisture in the air receiver must be purged regularly.
2) Air compressors must not deliver air at temperatures exceeding 110°C. Blast
cleaning equipment must be intrinsically safe and equipped with a remote shut-
off valve activated by a deadmans handle at the blasting nozzle.
3) Operators using air-operated equipment must have ventilated hoods or
headgear supplied with clean, cool air through a regulator filter to prevent
inhalation of blast cleaning residues.
4) Air Pressure for blast cleaning shall be min. 7 kg/cm² (100 psi).
5) The blasting nozzles must be venturi-style with tungsten or boron carbide liners,
with orifice sizes ranging from 3/16” to ¾”.
6) Grounding of hose and deadman handle for the hose
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > 5 Surface Preparation of SSPC-SP-13 NACE No.6 - > 5.3 Surface Preparation by Blast Cleaning > 5.3.2 Blast Cleaning Equipment

content:
5.3.3. Abrasives
1) Abrasives for cleaning carbon and low-alloy steels must meet ISO 8504-2
specifications. Suitable types include:
a) Chilled iron grit or shot,
b) Steel and malleable iron grit or shot, and
c) Non-metallic abrasives like aluminium oxide or garnet.
Note:
a) Sand blasting is prohibited in Refineries and Marine Oil Terminals, Crude Oil
Terminals etc.
b) Copper slag is used in such cases, except for all tank internals or as directed
by the owner.
c) For Tank internals, Steel and malleable iron grit or shot or garnet is to be
used.
d) CU slag grading:
i 0.2 to 2.4 mm - coarse sieve for paint & rust removal.
ii 0.4 to 1.5 mm – for general new surfaces.
iii Cu slag is specified as a general blasting material for BPCL, as this will
not create spark on metal surface while blasting. Cu slag is not reusable,
resulting in less/negligible surface contamination.
iv Abrasives must be free from oil, grease, moisture, and contaminants for
reuse, meeting SSPC AB1 requirements.
v Surface roughness must comply with the paint Manufacturer’s
recommendations, measured using instruments approved by the owner
and ISO 8503.
2) Surfaces should be blast-cleaned with the selected abrasive (Type a or b) at 7
kg/cm² pressure, maintaining constant nozzle size, distance, and angle for
uniform surface profile.
3) The combination of steel grits and shots is typically used in a 3:1 ratio. The
blasting nozzles must be venturi-style with tungsten or boron carbide liners, with
orifice sizes ranging from 3/16” to ¾”.
4) After blasting, surfaces must be clean, free of scale or rust, and exhibit a grey-
white metallic lustre.
5) Primer or first coat must be applied within four hours of surface preparation.
6) Outdoor blast cleaning is prohibited in bad weather conditions or when dew is
present on metal surfaces.
7) Steel or iron grit is commonly used as recyclable abrasives. When steel shot is
used, it shall be used together with steel grit. The shot content of such a mix
shall not exceed 67 %.
8) For blasting stainless steel, non-(free) iron containing abrasive shall be used,
e.g. Garnet or aluminium oxide.
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > 5 Surface Preparation of SSPC-SP-13 NACE No.6 - > 5.3 Surface Preparation by Blast Cleaning > 5.3.3 Abrasives

content:
9) Abrasives used for blast cleaning shall be free from oil, grease, moisture,
chloride contamination etc. and shall have crystalline silica not more than 1%.
10) Each batch of abrasive should be tested to check that the abrasive meets the
requirements as specified in the relevant ISO standard.
11) The conductivity of non-metallic abrasives to be used for stainless steels shall
be maximum 15 mS/m (1.5 µg/cm 2 ). For all other non-metallic abrasives, the
conductivity shall not exceed 25 mS/m (2.5 µg/cm 2 ).
12) Aluminium oxide or other abrasive (free from any chloride or iron/steel
contamination) shall be used for blasting stainless steel. Copper slag and sand
shall not be used.
5.4. Surface Preparation by Hand and Power Tool Cleaning
Manual cleaning using mechanical tools is suitable for removing mill scale and rust,
according to ISO 8504-3, only with owner approval for maintenance
coatings/painting.
5.5. Surface Preparation of Stainless Steel, Hot Galvanized Steel, and Non-Ferrous
Metals
1) For painting stainless steel, galvanized, or non-ferrous metal surfaces, light blast
cleaning with suitable abrasives is necessary to ensure paint system adhesion. Use
aluminium oxide or other chloride-free abrasives for stainless steel.
2) If blast cleaning is impractical, use solvent or steam cleaning with alkaline detergent
agents for surface preparation.
3) Contaminated galvanized surfaces with zinc salts or oil grease must first be cleaned
with fresh water or suitable solvent.
5.6. Surface Preparation of Concrete
The external surface of RCC to be painted shall be dry and clean. Any loose particle
of sand, cement, aggregate etc. shall be removed and surface shall be prepared as
per SSPC-SP-13.
6. Paint Application
The goal of painting/coating application is to create a continuous, highly adherent film with
uniform thickness over the substrate. Achieving this requires consideration of several
factors, including the type of coatings, weather conditions, and application methods. Paints
and other coating materials must be applied according to the Manufacturer’s instructions.
Painting requirements often depend on the substrate's temperature. Therefore, when
selecting a paint system, the operating temperature of the equipment must be considered.
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > 6 Paint Application

content:
6.1. Application Restrictions
The paint shall be applied in accordance with the Manufacturer’s product data sheet,
which includes the mix ratio, application method, use of thinners, and over-coating
times.
Following restrictions are given as recommended practices:
1) Coating application is prohibited during fog, mist, or rain or sandstorm.
2) Surface or Ambient Temperature: Coating application is prohibited when the
steel surface or ambient temperature is:
a) Below 10°C (50°F) or
b) Above 50°C (120°F) or
c) Less than 3°C (5°F) above the dew point.
3) Relative Humidity: Coating application is prohibited when relative humidity is
85% or above. Humidity and dew point readings must be taken by the Contractor
using a sling psychometric meter or digital humidity/dew point meter. Readings
should be taken before starting coating operations and every 4 hours during
application.
4) Moisture Deposition: Coating application is prohibited if there is moisture
deposition in the form of rain, condensation, or frost on the surface.
5) Light: Coating application is prohibited when available light is less than 500 lux.
6) Coatings should not be applied before the surface has been inspected and
preparatory work approved.
7) All sharp projections should be ground to a minimum 2 mm radius, and a thick
stripe coat applied at sharp edges.
8) If the surface of a paint coating is contaminated by condensation, rain, dust, or
other materials before it is dry to the touch, the paint must be removed, the
surface re-cleaned, and fresh paint applied as per this document.
9) Paints should not be applied within 50 mm of edges that will later be welded.
These weld areas should be taped (using masking tape) for 50 mm on either
side of the weld line. Extra coats of paint should be applied where the shape or
plane of application results in thin coatings, such as edges, welds, and corners.
Stripe coats should be applied to these areas first to be covered by the full coat.
10) Freshwater Blasting or Mopping or Low-Pressure Water Jetting should be
carried out on saltwater spray areas (e.g., Cooling tower area, Jetty, MOT, COT,
STF) before primer application to remove salt deposits on the metal surface. The
permissible chloride content for water used should be a maximum of 50 ppm.
The allowable salt content should be verified by conducting the Bresle Patch
Test as per ISO 8502-6 & 9 for external painting. Mopping can be repeated
before applying additional coats to remove suspected deposits on previous
coats.
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > 6 Paint Application > 6.1 Application Restrictions

content:
11) All surfaces should be free from oil, grease, dirt, salts, chlorides, sulphates, and
other foreign particles, and cleaned with high-pressure water washing. Tests for
soluble salts as per ISO 8502-9 should be carried out at one per twenty (20)
square meters or a minimum of 5 tests, whichever is more. The pH should be
neutral, and the maximum salt presence should be less than 5µg/cm² or
50mg/m². The frequency of testing should be increased as advised by the owner
based on site conditions.
12) NACE Certified inspector : Painting work must be supervised by a NACE
certified inspector for quality assurance. No painting work shall commence
without their presence. All Contractors responsible for painting are required to
adhere to this requirement.
13) Dehumidification system: Internal painting of tanks, vessels, or other
equipments necessitates the use of a dehumidification system to meet specified
conditions mentioned above or during monsoon period to improve the paint
quality and expediting painting works. Compliance with this requirement is
mandatory for all Contractors conducting internal painting operations. Refer
Annexure-1 on Specification for Dehumidification during Internal Blasting and
Painting of Tanks, Vessels & Pipelines. To ensure compliance, it is advised to
include separate Schedule of Rates (SOR) items in contracts specifically for the
mobilization of the dehumidification system. This will facilitate proper planning,
accountability, and verification of its deployment as per site conditions and paint
Manufacturer’s recommendations.
6.2. Mixing and Painting
6.2.1. Mixing & Thinning
All paint spraying equipment, including mixers, shall be thoroughly cleaned
before mixing of new materials. All coating materials shall be mixed and
thoroughly stirred in accordance with the instructions of the paint manufacturer.
Sufficient agitation to maintain good mixing shall be applied until the product is
used. If air is entrapped in the product during mixing/stirring, sufficient time
should be allowed for the air bubbles to escape before application.
6.2.2. Application of Paint/Primer:
a) Blast cleaned surface shall be coated with one complete application of
primer as soon as practicable but in no case later than 4 hrs. the same day
or as per the time frame stipulated in the technical data sheet of the paint,
whichever is earlier.
b) To the maximum extent practicable, each coat of material shall be applied
as a continuous film uniform thickness free of porous. Any spots or areas
missed in application shall be recoated and permitted to dry before the next
coat is applied. Applied paint should have the desired wet film thickness.
c) No coat shall be applied until the preceding coat has dried and cured as
recommended by Paint Manufacturer. Next coat shall be applied within the
time stipulated by the paint manufacturer. Before over coating, over-runs,
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > 6 Paint Application > 6.2 Mixing and Painting > 6.2.2 Application of Paint/Primer

content:
drips and smears shall be removed, and any coating damage or
imperfection shall be repaired. The material shall be considered dry for re-
coating when another coat can be applied without the development of any
film irregularities such as lifting or loss of adhesion of undercoats. Drying
time of the applied coat should not exceed maximum specified for it as a
first coat; if it exceeds the paint material has possibly deteriorated or mixing
is faulty.
d) No paint shall be forced dried under conditions, which will cause cracking,
wrinkling, blistering formation of pores, or detrimentally affect the conditions
of the paint. No drier should be added to paint on the job unless specifically
called for in the Manufacturer’s specification for the paint. Paint shall be
protected from rain, condensation and contamination until dry.
e) The Contractor shall protect all equipment, structures and any other areas
from mechanical damage, environmental damage, blockage or obstruction
and damage caused by over blasting, dripping paint, paint splashes and
overspray.
f) To minimize the risk of damage, no lifting, transportation, erection or
fabrication operations shall be done before the paint has been fully dried
and/or cured.
g) Abrasive blast cleaning and coating operations should be carried out during
daylight hours. However, grit blasting and coating may be carried out at
night providing the lighting and environmental conditions are acceptable.
Minimum lighting requirements for abrasive blasting, painting and
inspection are 500 lux.
1) Spray Application: Spraying is the required method of application. The Painting
Contractor shall supply the required range of spray tips, sizes and fan angles for
the work being undertaken. The spray pressure shall be uniform and the spray
pattern smooth.
2) Brush Application: Brush application may be done for Maintenance
Operations, in those cases where spray application may affect other plant,
equipment, property or personnel in or near the work site. Where the coating
product is specified as only being suitable for brush application. Manufacturer
shall confirm in writing that paint is suitable for brush application and will be able
to meet the guarantee period. Brush application shall be done only with
concurrence of Engineer In-Charge. Coatings applied by brush shall be smooth
and even.
3) Roller Application: Roller application shall only be allowed for steel floors and
for panels and equipment inside climate-controlled areas. Rollers shall be of
good quality to ensure uniformity of the applied coating.
4) Stripe Coat: Before spray application of each coat, irregular surfaces such as
sharp edges, welds, small brackets, interstices etc. shall be stripe-coated,
usually by brush, to ensure the specified film thickness is provided. Stripe coats
shall be applicable in all the painting systems. Different colour shades shall be
used for stripe coat.
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > 6 Paint Application > 6.2 Mixing and Painting > 6.2.2 Application of Paint/Primer

content:
5) Repair of Coating Defects: Damaged areas, defects in the coating system such
as over-runs, drips and smears that must be removed or areas with inadequate
dry film coating thickness shall be re-cleaned. The re-cleaning shall carry over
on to the sound coating for not less than 50 mm all round, the edges of which
shall be feathered.
After the surface cleaning, the specified coating system shall be re-applied. Any
additional coats shall blend in with the final coating at adjoining areas. Paint
Manufacturers shall be consulted if the existing coating requires abrading before
over-coating to obtain good bonding.
6) Only thinners specified by the paint Manufacturer shall be used.
6.3. Application Method
Coatings can be applied using various methods, with the primary ones being brush
and spray. Other methods include electrostatic spray, electrocoating, dipping, thermal
spray and fluidized bed techniques, which are for in-plant applications.
The method chosen depends on several factors. Brush application, suitable for oil-
based coatings, is the slowest but ideal for small or intricate areas and pitted surfaces.
Spray application is faster for large, flat surfaces but poses risks with toxic solvents
and fire hazards, making it less suitable for small, enclosed spaces. Cleanup is easier
with brushes and more complex with spray equipment.

7. Inspection and Testing of Painting Material & Painting Applications
7.1. Approved Manufacturers for Paint Materials
1) Painting Contractor shall procure paint from approved manufacturers listed below:
a) Advanced Paints
b) AkzoNobel Paints (or International Paints)
c) Asian Paints PPG
d) Berger Paints

2) Only paints from International Paint Manufacturers or Multi-National Paint
Manufacturing Companies (MNCs) are permitted for all internal coatings and
specialized external coatings (e.g., Glass flake epoxies, glass flake polyester
paints, phenolic novolac paints, Tank Liners, Inert polymer matrix coatings,
Temperature indicating paints etc.) with prior approval from BPCL Inspection.
3) Additional manufacturers may be considered by BPCL Inspection following
technical evaluation.
4) Prior approval from BPCL Inspection is mandatory before procuring paint materials,
as per the format in "Annexure-6". BPCL Inspection may request Proven Track
Record (PTR) based on material type and application areas.
5) All solvents used in the paint application shall be as recommended by the
manufacturer and shall be sourced from the same manufacturer to ensure
compatibility and performance.
7.2. Material & Inspection Requirements
1) All procured paint materials must be submitted with batch/test certificates for BPCL
Inspection approval. The remaining shelf life should be at least 30% of the total
duration at the time of inspection.
2) Material shall be procured well before in advance by Contractor/Owner and get it
inspected after procurement. Proper record shall be maintained while application
at site capturing the manufacturing and expiry dates for balance paint life. However,
it is preferred to have the materials from the fresh stock for better performance of
coatings.
3) It is recommended to submit data sheets for review and take prior approval from
BPCL Inspection about the paint material before ordering/procurement.
4) Manufacturer Test Certificate shall accompany all paint materials.
5) All zinc-based paints shall have minimum 85% of metallic Zinc by weight of total
solids on dry film. Glass flake epoxies shall have minimum 20% of glass flake
pigments in dry film by weight of total pigments.
6) During the major painting works, at least one batch zinc silicate paint testing shall
be carried out at third party Laboratory as per the instructions of BPCL Inspection
or Engineer In-Charge.

7) Inspection Engineer at his discretion may test paint formulations, if required at any
point of time.
8) All painting work shall be offered for stage wise inspection to BPCL
Inspection/Authorized Inspector/Consultants Painting QC. Refer Annexure-7 &
Annexure-8 for Paint material, surface preparation & application formats. Minimum
suggested stages of inspection shall be:
a) Surface Preparation
b) Primer application
c) Each coat of paint
9) All defects noticed during stages of inspection, shall be rectified free of cost by the
Contractor. The Contractor shall keep all records.
10) To avoid rework, it is preferable to take wet film thickness measurements during
painting to ascertain the adequacy and uniformity of thickness.
11) Dry film thickness (DFT) shall be taken on each coat after drying and curing of the
coat. DFT readings shall be taken using a non-destructive dry film thickness
instrument capable of storing the readings. Sufficient readings shall be taken
covering each coat prior to application of the following coat to ensure the correct
required DFT. The DFT indicated against each system shall be obtained by
applying one or more coat of the paint based on paint properties. The Contractor is
responsible to obtain the indicated DFT as per painting system specification.
12) DFT meter used shall be calibrated before each inspection and shall be witnessed
by the Inspector. It is the duty of the Inspector to satisfy him/herself with the
performance of the DFT meter.
13) Each Contractor shall deploy adequate number of NACE certified coating
inspectors to monitor and inspect the surface preparation, storage, handling,
mixing and application on substrate of the painting/coating and to ensure the
compliance of this specification.
14) The Inspection test plan which will be mandatory part of the contract shall be
prepared by the above qualified inspector and get approved by BPCL. The
Contractor ’s qualified coating inspector and BPCL shall sign an inspection report
as per approved inspection test plan. The report shall consist, as a minimum, of the
following in addition to the inspection parameters and acceptable criteria as given
in Table – 5.
a) Names of the Applicator and the responsible personnel.
b) Dates when work was carried out.
c) Equipment and techniques used.
d) Type and calibration of instruments used.
e) Weather and ambient conditions.
15) For coating different colour shades to be used for primer and intermediate coats,
for ease in monitoring & identification of number of coats.
16) Every painting Contractor shall mobilize painting inspection equipments like WFT
gauge, DFT meter, Surface Profile Hauge, Digital Hygrometer/Humidity

Additionally, painting should be performed under the following conditions:
1) Upon receiving inspection recommendations regarding damage to any painted
surface or deterioration of paint.
2) For any new structure or pipeline introduced due to modifications or MOC.
3) Internal painting of tanks, equipment and vessels should be done based on
inspection recommendations during shutdowns, available opportunities, or as per
the schedule.
8.2. Guarantee of Painting
1) The guarantee period shall match the painting frequency.
2) Paint materials must be sourced from approved manufacturers who will provide the
same guarantee as specified.
3) For all internal lining/painting works, the Manufacturer’s laboratory test statements
or performance certificates must be verified for specified services under
design/operating conditions including the performance test report. Materials should
be purchased only from approved manufacturers who meet these criteria.
4) For all tanks (crude as well as product tanks) painted during the M&I or Project stage,
a joint guarantee shall be obtained from the paint manufacturer and the applicator
(Contractor), in accordance with the requirements specified in Annexure-2 – “Paint
Performance Guarantee Requirements” . The formats provided in Annexure-3 –
“Proforma of Bank Guarantee” and Annexure -4 – “Format for Indemnity Bond” shall
be strictly followed.
5) To ensure effective management of the joint guarantee, all paints used for each tank
shall be procured from a single manufacturer including for internal as well as external
paints.
9. Storage
All paints and painting materials shall be stored in Contractor-arranged rooms approved
by the EIC. Fire prevention measures are essential. Ideally, storage buildings should
be separate from other structures, with a clear sign stating, “PAINT STORAGE –
HIGHLY INFLAMMABLE.”
1) Paint should be stored in well-ventilated areas.
2) Paint and thinner should be stored with visible manufacturer labels.
3) Paint stocks must be managed to ensure use within their effective period.
4) Implement prescribed protective measures and signage for storage areas.
5) Do not store paint exceeding the prescribed quantity.
6) Handle and store containers carefully to avoid damage.
7) Outdoor paint storage should be protected from damage using plastic or tin sheets.
8) Temporarily store only a day supply of paint in the working area, protected from
weather.
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > 9 Storage

content:
9) Paint requiring controlled temperatures should be stored in appropriate facilities
arranged by the Contractor at covered shed or air-conditioned facility.
10. Paint Monitoring System
All painting works must be marked with the completion date and Contractor's name at the
site/structure. This practice aids in identifying defects and serves as a basis for evaluating
the painting Contractor, paint system, and manufacturer for future projects.
11. Maintenance Painting
In an operating industry, regular and continuous maintenance painting is crucial. Surface
preparation is the most challenging part, especially since blasting is often not feasible in
running plants. Alternatives like power tool cleaning or manual cleaning are used, but they
do not remove old paint and rust thoroughly. This necessitates a reliable maintenance
coating that adheres to old surfaces. The following recommendations are provided for
various scenarios:
1) If the finish paint is intact but faded, clean the surface manually using water, a wire
brush, or fine emery cloth to remove dust and debris. Apply the finish paint as per the
coating system, ensuring the surface is dry.
2) If the finish paint or all coatings, including the primer, are damaged, use power tool
cleaning to St-3. If power tool cleaning is not possible, manually clean to ST-2, then
apply the complete paint scheme as applicable.
3) Measurement of DFT (Dry Film Thickness): During maintenance painting, measure the
DFT of the existing coating initially during surface inspection. Measure and record the
DFT after each subsequent coating application. Deduct the previous coating's DFT from
the total value to obtain the DFT of each layer.
12. Quality Control, Inspection and Testing
1) All painting materials including primers and thinners brought to site by Contractor for
application shall be procured directly from manufactures as per specifications and shall
be accompanied by Manufacturer ’ s test certificates. Paint formulations without
certificates are not acceptable.
2) The Contractor must produce test certificate from pre-qualified paint manufacturer for
various tests. The Inspection Dept./Engineer-In-Charge shall have the right to test wet
samples of paint at random for verifying quality of paint supplied. Contractor shall
arrange to have such tests, as instructed, performed at his cost at any one of the NABL
accredited laboratories under a TPI agency. Samples for the test will be drawn at
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > 12 Quality Control, Inspection and Testing

content:
random in presence Engineer-In-Charge or his representations. Following tests to be
carried out if called:
a) Specific Gravity
b) % Solids by weight (% Zinc content in case of inorganic or organic zinc primer)
c) Drying time (Touch dry & full curing)
d) Adhesion test on a test panel prepared at the site (ASTM D 6677).
Test methods for above tests shall be as per relevant ASTM or ISO Standard.
3) The painting work shall be subject to stage wise Inspection. Following stage-wise
inspection will be performed and Contractor shall offer the work for inspection and
approval of every stage before proceeding with the next stage. The record of inspection
shall be maintained in the prescribed formats given in this specification. Stages of
inspection are as follows:
a) Surface preparation
b) Primer application
c) Each coat of paint
12.1. Tests to be Carried Out During Surface Preparation:
a) Test for Presence of Oil/Grease and Contamination: The steel substrate after
degreasing as per SSPC-SP-1 shall be tested as per following procedure to
validate absence of oil and grease contamination:
• Visual inspection - continue degreasing until all visible signs of contamination
are removed.
• Conduct a solvent evaporation test by applying several drops or a small splash
of residue-free tri-chloromethane on the suspect area especially pitting, crevice
corrosion areas or depressed areas. An evaporation ring formation is indicative
of oil and grease contamination. Continue degreasing and inspection till test is
passed.
b) Tests for Surface Finish of Blasted Surface shall be done by visual inspection
using ISO 8501-1.
c) Dust Contamination Test: Clear cellophane tape test as per ISO 8502-3 shall
be used to confirm absence of dust on blasted surface.
d) Test for Presence of Soluble Salt as per method ISO 8502-9. In case salt
exceeds specified limit as per Table no. 5, the contaminated surface shall be
cleaned by method as per Annexure-C of ISO 12944-4 (water cleaning). After
cleaning, surface shall be retested for salt after drying.
e) Blast Profile Measurement: The angular profile depth measurement shall be
done as per ISO 8503-2. If profile is less than specified range, blasting shall
continue till greater specified depth profile is achieved.
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > 12 Quality Control, Inspection and Testing

content:
f) Tests for blasting media: Blasting Media (For every fresh batch of media and
one random test during blasting). Blasting Media shall be visually inspected for
absence of contamination and debris using 10 X magnification.
• Inspection for the absence of oil contamination shall be conducted using
following procedure:
o Fill a small clean 200 ml bottle half full of abrasive.
o Fill the bottle with potable water, cap and shake the bottle.
o Inspect water for oil film/slick. If present, the blasting media is not to
be used.
• Soluble salt contamination if suspected shall be verified by method ASTM D
4940. If present, media to be replaced.
• Clean blasting equipment, especially pot and hoses, and then replace
blasting media and retest.
g) Test for Blasting Air (Once Daily before start of blasting and once at random
during blasting). The air for blasting shall be free from moisture and oil. The
compressor air shall be checked for oil and water contamination per ASTM D
4285.
In addition to above, record should include type of shop primer already applied on
equipment e.g. zinc silicate or zinc rich epoxy or zinc phosphate. Any defect noticed
during the various stages of inspection shall be rectified by the Contractor to the entire
satisfaction of Engineer-In-Charge before proceeding further. Irrespective of the
inspection, repair and approval at intermediate stages of work, Contractor shall be
responsible for rectifying any defects found during final inspection/guarantee
period/defect liability period as defined in general conditions of the contract. Dry film
thickness (DFT) shall be checked and recorded after application of each coat and extra
coat of paint should be applied to make-up the DFT specified without any extra cost to
OWNER, the extra coat should have prior approval of Engineer-in-charge.
12.2. Final Inspection of Finished Coating:
a) Coating DFT & WFT Check: DFT measurement shall be as per ISO 2808. Type-II
electronic gauge shall be used as described in ASTM D7091. DFT gauge
calibration, adjustment of gauge coating thickness, frequency and number of
coating thickness measurement shall be as per ASTM D7091 and SSPC-PA2.
Measured DFT shall have a tolerance as per SSPC-PA-2 level 3 of the dry film
thickness, specified in this specification. Once in every layer of coating application,
spot checks of WFT shall be carried out during the coating application according to
ISO 2808, Method No. 1A- Comb gauge. This is to ensure that film thickness is
being achieved as per the specification. The frequency of WFT check shall be one
per 10 m 2 .
b) Adhesion Testing: Adhesion of the primer to the steel substrate and inter-coat
adhesion of the subsequent coat(s) after curing for at least a week after application
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > 12 Quality Control, Inspection and Testing > 12.2 Final Inspection of Finished Coating:

content:
of the topcoat shall be examined by a knife test in accordance with ASTM D6677.
For the knife test, if the rating is better than 8, the adhesion is considered
acceptable. The adhesion is destructive, and tested areas shall be repaired
afterward using the spot repair procedure. Alternatively, the applicator may perform
the adhesion test on a steel panel coated using the same surface preparation and
coating application procedure as the work piece. Adhesion testing shall be carried
out for each component at least once per 200 m 2 of coated surface.
c) Holiday Detection Check: Holiday testing of non-conductive coatings shall be
carried out in accordance with NACE SP0188. For immersion, buried & under
insulation services, 100% of coated area shall be inspected for holidays. For
atmospheric exposure, 10% of coated area which must include weld seams, corners
and edges to be holiday tested. Voltage at which test is to be carried out will depend
upon DFT of coating being tested and shall be as per NACE SP0188. Any holiday
is unacceptable and should be marked and repaired immediately.

13.6. General Notes for Paint Material Tables:
• Note-A: Covering capacity and DFT achieved per coat depends on method of
application. Covering capacity specified above is theoretical. For estimation of
actual quantity of paints required, include the losses during application. Minimum
specified DFT should be maintained in any case.
• Note-B: All primers and finish coats should be ambient temperature curing and air
drying unless otherwise specified.
• Note-C: All paints shall be applied in accordance with Manufacturer’s instructions
for surface preparation, intervals, curing and application. Wherever a deviation is
noticed from the specification in manufacturer data sheet, more stringent one
between the data sheet and the specification shall prevail e.g. if this specification
recommends Sa 2 ½ and the manufacturer data sheet requires Sa 3, the surface
preparation shall be done as per Sa 3. However, in another case if this specification
requires the surface preparation of Sa 2 ½ and the manufacturer data sheet

content:
recommends only Sa 2 as minimum, the surface preparation shall be done as per
Sa 2 ½.
• Note-D: Technical data sheets of all paints shall be submitted for pre-procurement
approval of proposed paint materials.
• Note-E: Internationally recognized & acceptable testing method shall be used for
lab testing wherever testing standards are not mentioned.
• Note-F: Touch dry, hard dry, pot life, full cure period, & over coating interval shall
be as per Manufacturer’s data sheets, and no testing is required. Slight variation in
the values of these parameters along with weight per litre may be permissible with
the discretion of Engineer-In-Charge/BPCL Inspection.
• Note-G: Temperature Resistance Test (ASTM D2485) shall be carried out for
minimum required temperature resistance indicated.
• Note-H: Wherever ASTM D 2485 Method B is applicable, test results of the panels
subjected to salt spray (ASTM B 117) after muffle furnace exposure shall be
submitted for pre-qualification purpose.
• Note-I: F6-C shall be suitable for drinking water service and should have potable
water certification from competent authority.
• Note-J: Maximum overcoat interval shall be as per Paint Manufacturer’s datasheet.
• Note-K: For F-16, BF-4 – The paint system should pass ISO 12944 requirement for
ageing resistance. Third Party Certificate to be provided and prior approval shall be
taken. Holiday testing is not reliable due to presence of metallic pigments.
• Note-L: For BT-1 Paint - After hard dry time, fill the equipment with water and raise
the temp up to 100 0 C and hold it for 8 hrs for complete curing of product.
• Note-M: Prior to procurement of paint material, Contractor shall propose suitable
product fulfilling technical requirements as per above material specification, and
submit their datasheet, Proven Track Record (if available) and third-party test
certificate for review and approval.
• Note-N: For BF-4 Paint - The paint should pass ISO 12944 requirements for Ageing
Resistance. Third Party Certificate should be provided & prior approval shall be
taken.
• Note-O: For all internal lining and painting works, it is essential that paint
manufacturers submit comprehensive documentation for evaluation. This includes
the Manufacturer’s laboratory test reports, laboratory paint performance certificates,
third-party test reports, and Proven Track Record. These documents must be
reviewed in addition to the material test certificates, which should confirm suitability
for the specified service conditions, including operating temperature and
environment. Approval of paint materials shall be strictly limited to approved
manufacturers who meet all the above requirements.
• Note-P: For Brand Names Refer Annexure-11
• Note- Q : F-12 shall be ambient temperature curing type (moisture cured).

14. Selection of Coating Systems:
This section outlines the systematic approach for selecting appropriate coating
systems for steel structures, considering environmental severity, service conditions,
design temperature, and plant location. The selection process follows ISO 12944-2
guidelines for C5 environments (for external environment), ensuring very high
durability.
14.1. Procedure for Paint System Selection:
The paint system to be applied for a specific job shall be arrived at sequentially as
given below:
1) Determine the Type of Work:
• Check if the job is for a new project or recoating (maintenance coating).
• Identify whether the surface is insulated or uninsulated, underground or
aboveground.
• Note the design temperature and type of surface material (metallurgy).
2) Refer to the Appropriate Table:
• Use Table-13 for general surfaces (excluding tanks).
• Use Table-15 for internal coating systems of tanks.
• Use Table-16 for external coating systems of tanks.
3) Select the Paint System Number:
• Based on the above classification, choose the correct system number from
the relevant table (13, 15, or 16).
4) Review Detailed Paint System Description:
• Go to Table-14 & specific system no. to find:
• Surface preparation method applicable.
• Paint codes and detailed descriptions of each system.
• Dry Film Thickness (DFT) required for each coat.

14.4. General Notes for Paint System Description Tables:
• Note-1: The Blast Cleaning Abrasives for SS and Alloy surfaces shall be SS Grits/Shots or Aluminium Oxide Grits/Shots.
• Note-2: In case of Direct-To-Metal Coatings/Paints, Manufacturer Recommendations to be followed for Primer selection or
Primer not to be applied if not recommended by Manufacturer. However, overall DFT shall be same as specified.
• Note-3: For touch up on F-9 (Inorganic Zinc Ethyl Silicate Primer) or in case DFT of applied F-9 is low, in such cases Zinc
Phosphate Primer (P-6) shall be used. If DFT of F-9 is lower than 40 microns then one coat of F-8 shall be applied without any
additional cost. EIC may ask to re-blast the surface and re-apply F-9 if DFT is observed marginally low. No overcoating using F-
9 is to be done.
• Note-4: F-9 Applied surfaces should be fully cured before application of subsequent coat and shall be checked by MEK Test
(In-line with ASTM D4752). Spraying of water to the primed surface should be done in case the surfaces are not getting cured.
Manufacturer recommendations to be followed for the same.
• Note-5: In case of F-9 applied on external surfaces (cold surfaces), subsequent coat should be applied in form of mist coat (40-
50% thinning) followed by a full coat to ensure that the porosity of F-9 surfaces filled.
• Note-6: The application and repair of pre-erection/pre-fabrication and shop primer given in above tables shall be done for all
the items to be painted. In case the damages of primer are severe and spread over large area as per EIC discretion, entire
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > PART G External Surfaces > 14.4 General Notes for Paint System Description Tables:

content:
primer shall be removed by blasting to achieve SSPC-SP-10 and surfaces shall be suitably primer coated as per relevant coating
system as advised.
• Note-7: Repair of the damaged areas of galvanized coatings due to welding during erection shall be carried out as per
recommended practice ISO 1461, using cold galvanizing spray process. Organic paint systems are not acceptable for the repair.
After repair of damaged galvanized coating by cold galvanization (P-7), the repaired area shall be top coated with paint system
as per system 23A or 23B.
• Note-8: Spot Repair of Damaged Primer
a. Where pre-erection/shop primer has been damaged at isolated localized spots during handling and transportation or after
erection/welding, its repair shall be done as given below.
b. Surface Preparation: Quickly remove the primer from damaged area by mechanical scraping and emery paper conforming
to SSPC-SP-3 to expose the white metal. Blast clean the surface, if possible. Feather the primed surface, over the intact
adjacent surface surrounding the damaged area, by emery paper.
c. Primer P6 (Zinc Phosphate Primer) shall be applied wherever damage was observed on pre-erection / prefabrication or
shop primer.
d. If damaged areas are found to be extensive and spread over large areas (as required by Engineer-In-Charge), then entire
pre-erection/pre-fabrication/shop primer shall be removed by blasting to achieve SSPC-SP-10 and entire blasted surface
shall be primed again with F-8/F-9 or F-16, as applicable, for the intended design temperature. If the prepared surface
lies adjacent to a sound coated surface, the surface preparation shall overlap the coated surface by at least 50 mm. The
remainder of existing coated surface shall be properly protected with shields or screens to prevent any possible damage
to the coating.
e. No over coating of F-9 on existing F-9 is allowed
• Note-9: F-12 shall be ambient temperature curing type (moisture cured).
• Note-10: Surface profile for F-9 shall be 30-40 µm
• Note-11: For System-33A & 33B – If painting is carried out on site within 100 m radius of cooling towers:
a. Pipelines, Pipe racks, Structures, Tanks etc. in the Salt water/Contaminated Raw water spray area shall be covered from
the top & sides while doing painting work at site to avoid deposition of salt after surface preparation.
b. Fresh water blasting using hydro jetting shall be carried out on Salt water/Contaminated Raw water spray areas (Like
cooling tower areas, Jetty, MOT, COT etc.) before application of primer to remove the salts deposited on surface.
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > PART G External Surfaces > 14.4 General Notes for Paint System Description Tables:

content:
c. The permissible Chloride content for water used shall be 50 ppm maximum. The allowable salt content shall be ensured
by conducting Bresle Patch Test as per ISO 8502-6 & 9 for external. The frequency & acceptable limit shall be as per
table no. 5.
d. Mopping to be repeated before application of other coats to remove salts deposited on previous coats, if such deposits
are suspected.
• Notes-12: For System-6
a. Carry out shot blasting of the surface to Sa 2.5 finish.
b. Apply one coat of synthetic primer conforming to AWWA 203/2003 and IS 15337/2003 standards. The primer shall be
compatible with the hot applied tape.
c. Use 2 layers of 2 mm thick each hot applied anti-corrosive tape conforming to AWWA 203 and IS 15337 standards.
d. Both the primer and tape shall be of one manufacturer for compatibility.
e. Ensure that the tape is tightly wound on the pipe.
f. Spirally wrap the self-adhesive tape on the primed surface with 25 mm overlap.
g. The length of the tape shall be kept sufficiently extra to complete the length of the pipeline.
h. The spiral joint if any shall have 25 mm overlap.
i. The tape coated pipes & field joint coated area shall be holiday tested using portable holiday tester using 12000 volts.
j. Weld joints shall be coated only after hydro test.
k. For exposed (above ground) section of Tape coating – A UV resistant paint (bituminous based aluminium paint or similar
as recommended by manufacturer) shall be applied.
• Note-13: For any paint system- In case the primer is not recommended by the paint manufacturer, then total DFT of the finished
paint system shall be inclusive of primer DFT as specified against each system.
• Note-14 : For system 20, 22 & 25 – PTR of last 10 years performance to be taken for similar service / application areas for new
product approvals.

14.7. General Notes for Tanks Internal & External Painting System Tables:
• Note-i: All paint coating application including primer for tanks shall be carried out at field after erection and completion of welding.
• Note-ii: For Underside of Bottom Plate:
a. Painting shall be carried out before laying the bottom plate for tanks with Non-Post Weld Heat Treatment (PWHT).
b. For tanks with PWHT, painting shall be carried out after PWHT.
c. In case tank is not lifted during PWHT then painting shall be applied before laying the bottom plate.
• Note-iii: Stripe coat is must for weld area, edges and corners.
• Note-iv: Over zinc silicate, intermediate coat to be applied in mist coat and full coat technique.
• Note-v: All sharp edges to be grinded well before painting.
• Note-vi: Pitted area having width greater than 2 mm or depth greater than 5 mm to be filled with solvent less polyamine adduct
cured epoxy.
• Note-vii: Camouflaging not to be done.
• Note-viii: External floating roof tanks top shell internal painting shall extend upto 100 mm above top of shunt of secondary seal
marked at safe filling height during hydro test.
• Note-ix: Aluminium surfaces shall not to be painted.
• Note-x: Before changing of tank service resistivity of the painting system to be verified.
• Note-xi: In tank history details of paint Manufactures with brand name to be mentioned. These would facilitate defects
observations and would also be basis for evaluation of the painting Contractor/paint system and manufacturer for future works.

15. Pre-Qualification/Acceptance Criteria for Paint Coating System
15.1. Pre-Qualification of Paint Coating Manufacturer and Their Products
Paint manufacturer meeting the following requirements shall be considered by the
Contractor for supply of the paint products.
1) Manufacturer should have been in continuous business of paint coating
formulation and manufacturer for at least past 5 years.
2) Manufacturer should possess past experience of supplying products to
hydrocarbon, petrochemical, fertilizer, chemical processing, offshore or heavy
industry in the past 5 years.
3) Coating manufacturer should have supplied at least 10000 litres of an individual
product to hydrocarbon, petrochemical, fertilizer, chemical processing, offshore
or heavy industry.
4) The Manufacturer’s manufacturing procedure & QA/QC system shall meet ISO
9001 requirements and preferably should possess ISO 14000 certificate.
5) The Quality Control set up should be manned by qualified paint technologists
whose biodata should be sent along with quality control organization chart.
The Proven Track Record of the manufacturers as mentioned above shall be
substantiated by providing relevant documents for their review and acceptance. This
shall include Work Orders, Purchase Orders and ISO 9001 certification covering all
the requirements mentioned from bullets 1) to 5) above.
Contractor shall procure the paint materials from the qualified manufacturer meeting
above requirements and after obtaining prequalification testing approval as per
requirements mentioned in clause below.
15.2. Pre-Qualification Testing Procedure:
The paint manufacturer engaged by the Contractor shall carry out the tests in NABL
accredited government laboratories or in suggested list of laboratories below, as a
part of qualification. Paint manufacturer shall provide the paint samples to laboratory
for testing of the parameters mentioned as material properties against each
materials technical requirement and tests on coating systems of this specification.
The testing laboratory will confirm the compliance of the paint material with respect
to the acceptance criteria mentioned in the respective tables.
Coloured photographs of coated test panels should be taken before and after the
test and should be enclosed along with test report. Contractor shall furnish these
test certificates along with all necessary supporting documents/information for
approval/acceptance. The paint manufacturer will be qualified and approved for
supply of paints after review/assessment of the submissions made by the
Contractor. Test certificates which are more than 3 years old will not be considered.

16. Method of Sampling & Dispatch for Laboratory Testing
1) Samples of coating materials should be submitted to the laboratory in sealed
containers with Batch No. and Test Certificate on regular format of
Manufacturer’s testing laboratory.
2) All test panels should be prepared by testing laboratory. Surface preparation for
a system shall be done in accordance with this specification. For individual
products testing, minimum shall be Sa 2.5. Colour photographs of test panels
should be taken before and after the test and should be enclosed along with test
report. Sample Batch No. and Manufacturer’s Test Certificate should be
enclosed along with the report. Test report must contain details of observation
and rusting if any, as per the testing code.
3) Manufacturers should intimate BPCL, details of sample submitted for testing,
name of testing agency, date, and contact personnel of the testing agency.
17. Standard for Colour Coding of Pipelines/Equipments
Non-Uniformity of colour coding of pipelines in industrial installations causes
faulty manipulation of valves, cutting of lines, welding online carrying hazardous
or inflammable material etc. resulting into the destruction of property and injury
to personnel. Uniformity of colour promotes greater safety, lessens the chances
of error and warns against the hazards involved in the handling of material inside
the pipelines.
Identification of the contents of the pipelines is achieved by imposing suitable
colour bands on the ground colour. Letter writing as a mode of identification is
also recommended for chemical industry, as this will reduce the possibility of
mistakes in identification. Lettering may include the contents by name, chemical
formula or unmistakable and standard abbreviations.
This standard prescribes the colour scheme for the identification of the contents
of pipelines carrying fluids in the BPCL Refineries located at Mumbai, Kochi &
Bina. This standard is not applicable to pipelines buried underground or used for
electrical services like conduits.
A. Paints: Appropriate quality of paints shall be used for colour marking. It is
recommended that the paints used should produce a glossy finish.
B. Colours: To identify the contents of the pipelines, many colour shades are
required. The references of colour are taken from IS 2379:1990 “Pipelines –
Identification – Colour Code” and to standardization of colour shades RAL colour
are selected which are matching to the max. extent with the colours as per
IS:2379. The ground colours are selected for the various group of services taken
from the IS Code to identify the basic nature of fluid carried and distinguishing
one liquid from other. The various ground colours are given in Table-18.

• One kind or condition of a fluid from another kind or condition of the
same fluid OR
• One fluid from another but belonging to the same group.

• At battery limits.
• Intersection points & change of direction points in piping.
• Midway of piping section, near valves, across culverts.
• At 50 m interval on long stretch pipes.
• At starting and termination points.

content:
Annexure 1: Technical Specification for Dehumidification During
Internal Blasting and Painting of Tanks, Vessels & Pipelines
This specification outlines the essential requirements for dehumidification during internal
blasting and painting of tanks, vessels, and pipelines. It ensures optimal conditions by
controlling humidity, dew point, and temperature, preventing moisture-related issues like
condensation and flash rust. Adhering to these guidelines enhances coating adhesion,
promotes faster curing, and ensures long-term protection against corrosion.
An adequately designed dehumidification system shall be employed during internal
blasting and coating operations in tanks, vessels, and pipelines. The system must meet
the following criteria to ensure optimal surface preparation and coating performance:
1. Relative Humidity Control :
The Relative Humidity (RH) inside the tank, vessel, or pipeline shall be
maintained at less than 60% throughout the entire blasting and painting process,
including during nighttime hours.
2. Dew Point and Surface Temperature :
To prevent condensation and flash rusting, the air dew point must be maintained
at least 3°C below the surface temperature of the material surface being blasted
and painted.
3. Moisture Removal Rate :
The minimum grain drop (Moisture Removal Rate) across the dehumidifier shall
be determined based on the volume of the area being dehumidified. Calculations
shall be performed using a psychometric chart to ensure proper dehumidification
capacity.
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > ANNEXURE 1 Technical Specification for Dehumidification During > 3 Moisture Removal Rate :

content:
4. Partitioning of Large Tanks :
For larger diameter tanks, the dehumidification process shall be applied to ¼ of
the tank at a time. For other tanks, up to ½ of the tank may be dehumidified at
once, with the area divided using suitable partitions such as scaffolding, tarpaulin
sheets, or GI sheets.
5. Air Change Rate :
The required dehumidified air quantity shall be calculated based on the tank
capacity, with the following air change rates applied:
o Up to 2000 KL: 3 air changes per hour
o 2000 - 10,000 KL: 2 air changes per hour
o 10,000 - 35,000 KL: 1 air change per hour
o Above 35,000 KL: 0.75 air changes per hour
6. Temperature Control :
The internal temperature of the tank shall be maintained between 25°C to 35°C
during blasting and painting. A condensing unit may be used in conjunction with
the dehumidifiers and blowers to achieve this temperature range.
7. Continuous Dehumidification :
Dehumidification shall be maintained round-the-clock during the entire surface
preparation and painting process to accelerate paint curing. The dehumidifier
must not be stopped until the priming of the blasted surface is fully completed to
the satisfaction of the Engineer-In-Charge (EIC) or Inspection team.
8. Equipment Breakdown :
In the event of a dehumidifier failure, the Contractor must replace the equipment
at their own risk and cost. Any unfinished work due to the failure must be repeated
as necessary.
9. Performance Compliance :
The EIC or Inspection team reserves the right to disallow the use of any
dehumidifier if its performance does not meet the specified requirements. The
Contractor shall, at no additional cost to the owner, replace the equipment to
ensure compliance with the specified standards.

Annexure 2: Paint Performance Guarantee Requirements
Guidelines for Painting System Execution and Guarantees:
1. Comprehensive Execution by Approved Vendors : To ensure BPCL receives
full value for its investment in the painting system, the entire scope of painting
work should be entrusted to paint manufacturers or applicators who are approved
by the respective paint manufacturer. To ensure clear accountability of both the
paint applicator (Contractor) and the paint manufacturer, it is recommended that
only one Manufacturer’s paint system to be used for any given equipment. This
approach helps to maintain consistency in product performance and simplifies
responsibility in case of any defects or failures.
2. Compliance with Specifications : The Contractor or paint manufacturer must
guarantee that the chemical and physical properties of all paint materials used
strictly adhere to the specifications outlined in this document.
3. Rectification of Defects During Guarantee Period : Any defects arising within
the guaranteed lifespan of the paint system, whether due to substandard
materials or improper application must be rectified by the paint manufacturer or
Contractor at no additional cost to BPCL.
4. Duration of Guarantee : The guarantee period shall be as specified in Table
II.1 below.

5. Submission of Guarantees:
All guarantees must be submitted to BPCL in the standard format provided within
this specification. The guarantee shall comprise two components:
i) Performance Guarantee: The painting Contractor shall provide a bank
guarantee valid for two years from the date of job completion. This guarantee
must be equivalent to 10% of the total contract value for the painting work and
will serve as the retention security for the initial two-year period.
ii) Corporate Guarantee : For the remaining guarantee period, a corporate
guarantee must be submitted. This corporate guarantee must be jointly
signed by both the painting Contractor and the paint manufacturer, based on
a formal agreement between the two parties.
iii) These guarantees must be submitted either before the commencement of the
painting job or immediately after its completion. Payment will be released only
upon submission of the required guarantees.
6. Joint Inspections and Maintenance During Guarantee Period:
During the guarantee period, the paint manufacturer shall conduct joint
inspections with BPCL engineers as follows:
• Every six months during the first two years
• Annually thereafter for the remaining guarantee period
All necessary repairs identified during these inspections shall be carried out by
the paint manufacturer at their own cost. The extent of corrosion and coating
performance shall be jointly monitored and documented during these inspections.

1.0 Framework and Purpose of This Contract
This contract defines what constitutes a defect in the coating system and what are
the rights, obligations and liabilities of the parties in case of defect.
2.0 The Guarantee
a. The "GUARANTOR" hereby confirms having carefully examined the coating
system supplied by the "OWNER" in the tender document. Considering all
technical aspects & climatic conditions prevailing at the location, the
"GUARANTOR" confirm that specified coating system are fully suitable for
the desired services on mild steel vertical oil storage tanks, steel structures
and steel pipelines & other equipments etc. at the location.
b. The "Paint Manufacturer / Applicator (Contractor) ” hereby confirms that they
will ensure thorough surface preparation as per specified Swedish Standards
and BPCL paint specification to have desired surface profile before
application of paint system on the steel surface.
c. The "GUARANTOR" hereby agrees to provide all latest codes, SSPC
guidelines for painting, providing surface profile comparators, wet & dry film
thickness measuring gadgets and other painting inspection kits for inspection
by "OWNER" conforming to latest SSPC guidelines for painting inspection.
d. The "Paint Manufacturer / Applicator (Contractor )” hereby confirms to
provide Dry Film Thickness (DFT) of paint specified for every coat. If, actual
DFT of a particular coat is beyond the permissible limits of acceptance of
variation in DFT, the "GUARANTOR" undertakes to blast the painted surface
again and apply paint system to achieve the specified DFT for every coat.
e. The "GUARANTOR" hereby warrants that the coating system shall remain
free from defects, including but not limited to chalking, cracking, alligatoring,
flaking, peeling, delamination, rusting, blistering, wrinkling, edge failure, and
failure around welds, throughout the duration of the Guarantee Period. The
Guarantor further guarantees the performance and integrity of the coating
system for the entirety of the Guarantee Period.
f. "Performance" means that there will be no defective areas of the coating
system during the Guarantee period.
g. The "GUARANTOR" (Paint Manufacturer / Applicator (Contractor)) hereby
agree that guarantee period of coating system will commence on the
completion of painting work as detailed in the Agreement No. ___________
dated _____________. This corporate guarantee shall commence on

content:
Annexure 5: Procedure for Measurement of Dry Film Coating
Thickness
Definitions:
DFT – Dry Film Thickness
Gauge Reading – A single DFT reading at one point.
Spot – A circle of 4 cm diameter
Spot Measurement - Average of acceptable three-gauge readings within a spot (Circle
of 4 cm diameter)
Spot Measurement Procedure:
Minimum of three (3) gauge readings shall be made for each spot measurement of either
the substrate or the coating. Repeated gauge readings, even at points close together,
may differ due to small surface irregularities of the coating and the substrate. For each
new gauge reading move the probe to a new location within 4 cm diameter circle defining
the spot. Discard any unusually high or low reading that cannot be repeated consistently.
Take the average of the acceptable gauge readings as the spot measurement.
Required Number of Measurements for Conformance to a Thickness
Specification.
Number of Measurements:
Make five (5) spot measurements spaced randomly over each 10 m 2 (100 ft 2 ) area to be
measured. No. of spot measurement beyond five for an area of 10 m 2 can be increased
as decided by BPCL. The five spot measurements shall be made for each 10 m 2 ( 100
ft 2 ) of area as follows:
a. For structures not exceeding 30 m 2 (300 ft 2 ) in area, each 10 sq. m 2 (100 ft 2 ) area
shall be measured.
b. For structures from 30 m 2 to 100 m 2 (1000 ft 2 ) in area, three 10 m 2 (100 ft 2 ) areas
shall be randomly selected and measured.
c. For structures exceeding 100 m 2 (1000 ft 2 ) in area, the first 10 m 2 (1000 ft 2 ) shall be
measured as stated in (b) above and for increment thereof or each additional 100
m 2 (1000 ft 2 ) of area, one 10 m 2 (1000 ft 2 ) area shall be randomly selected and
measured.
d. If the dry film thickness for any 10 m 2 (100 ft 2 ) area as mentioned in (b) & (c) above
is not in compliance with the requirements of (e) & (f) as mentioned below, then
additional measurements must be made to isolate the nonconforming area.
breadcrumb: Technical Specification of Paint Coatings for BPCL Refineries > ANNEXURE 5 Procedure for Measurement of Dry Film Coating Thickness > Spot Measurement Procedure

content:
Specifying Thickness:
Both a maximum and a minimum DFT should be specified for the coating. If a maximum
thickness value is not explicitly specified, the specified thickness shall be minimum.
a. Minimum Thickness: The average of five spot measurements for each 10 m 2 (100
ft 2 ) area shall not be less than the specified minimum DFT. The average of three-
gauge readings at any single spot measurement in any 10 m 2 (100 ft 2 ) area shall
not be less than 80% of the specified minimum DFT. If the average of five spot
measurements for a given 10 m 2 (100 ft 2 ) area meets or exceeds the specified
minimum DFT and any spot measurement does not meet 80% of the specified
minimum DFT, additional spot measurements may be made to define the non-
conforming areas.
b. Maximum Thickness: The average of five spot measurements for each 10 m 2 (100
ft 2 ) area shall not be more than the specified maximum DFT. No single spot
measurement in any 10 m 2 (100 ft 2 ) area shall be more than 120% of the specified
minimum DFT. If the average of the five spot measurements of a given 10 m 2 (100
ft 2 ) area meets or falls below the specified maximum DFT, but one or more spot
measurements is more than 120% of the specified minimum DFT, additional spot
measurements may be made to define the non-conforming area.
